disease.name: Acute Cholecystitis
disease.note: Acute inflammation of the gallbladder, usually due to cystic duct obstruction by gallstones. Murphy sign is pathognomonic — arrest of inspiration during RUQ palpation. Calculous cholecystitis is most common; acalculous occurs in critically ill patients. In Kenya, gallstone disease is rising with increasing obesity and Western dietary patterns.
presentingComplaints: Severe pain in the right upper abdomen
Pain that spreads to the right shoulder or back
Tenderness when pressing on the right side
pathophysiologyDetail: Cystic duct obstruction: gallstone impaction at the neck of the gallbladder or in the cystic duct → mechanical obstruction → bile stasis → increased intraluminal pressure → gallbladder distension → wall edema → venous and lymphatic compression → ischemia

Inflammatory cascade: obstructed bile → mucosal injury → release of phospholipase A2, prostaglandins (PGE2, PGI2), and leukotrienes → neutrophil infiltration → edema and wall thickening → progressive inflammation

Bacterial superinfection: initially sterile chemical inflammation; secondary bacterial invasion via translocation across compromised mucosa or ascending from CBD → E. coli (most common), Klebsiella, Enterococcus, Bacteroides → empyema (pus in gallbladder)

Gangrenous cholecystitis: progressive ischemia from sustained high intraluminal pressure → full-thickness necrosis of gallbladder wall → risk of perforation; occurs in 20-30% of acute cholecystitis patients with delayed presentation (>72 hours); higher risk in diabetics and elderly

Emphysematous cholecystitis: gas-forming organisms (Clostridium perfringens, E. coli) → gas in gallbladder wall and lumen on imaging; predominantly in diabetic men; 25% mortality — surgical emergency requiring urgent cholecystectomy

Perforation of gallbladder: stage I (acute free perforation with peritonitis), stage II (subacute pericholecystic abscess), stage III (chronic cholecystoenteric fistula); stage I has highest mortality

Mirizzi syndrome: gallstone impacted in the cystic duct or gallbladder neck → external compression of the common hepatic duct → obstructive jaundice; type I (extrinsic compression without fistula), type II-IV (cholecystocholedochal fistula with varying degrees of bile duct destruction)

Gallstone ileus: cholecystoduodenal fistula from a large gallstone eroding through the gallbladder wall → stone passes into duodenum → impacts at ileocecal valve → mechanical small bowel obstruction; Rigler's triad: (1) pneumobilia, (2) ectopic calcified stone, (3) small bowel obstruction on imaging

Acalculous cholecystitis: gallbladder stasis from prolonged NPO, TPN, mechanical ventilation, or critical illness → bile concentration → mucosal ischemia → bacterial translocation; occurs in 5-10% of ICU patients; higher mortality due to underlying critical illness

Biliary sludge and microlithiasis: cholesterol crystals, calcium bilirubinate, and mucus aggregates → sludge that can obstruct cystic duct → acute cholecystitis without visible stones; identified on endoscopic ultrasound or microscopy of bile; more common in pregnancy, rapid weight loss, TPN

Cholesterolosis (strawberry gallbladder): cholesterol esters accumulate in macrophages within the gallbladder mucosa → yellow spots on red mucosa (strawberry appearance); benign but may cause biliary-type pain; does not predispose to cholecystitis unless associated with stones

Porcelain gallbladder: extensive calcification of gallbladder wall → chronic inflammation; historically associated with gallbladder cancer (10-25% risk); current evidence suggests lower risk; prophylactic cholecystectomy recommended

Rising gallstone disease in East Africa: dietary transition to high-fat, processed foods in urban Kenya; obesity prevalence increasing (27% in Kenyan women); more women presenting with gallstone-related cholecystitis; laparoscopic cholecystectomy now available at most provincial hospitals

Gallbladder polyp: >10 mm polyps have significant malignant potential (>50% risk of carcinoma in polyps >15 mm); cholecystectomy indicated for polyps >10 mm regardless of symptoms; smaller polyps (<5 mm) can be followed with surveillance ultrasound at 6-12 month intervals

Gallbladder cancer pathogenesis: chronic inflammation (especially in porcelain gallbladder, cholelithiasis >20 years duration, and large gallstones >3 cm) → mucosal dysplasia → carcinoma in situ → invasive adenocarcinoma; 80% are incidentally discovered at cholecystectomy in Kenya due to lack of screening

Post-cholecystectomy syndrome: 10-30% of patients have persistent or new symptoms after cholecystectomy. Causes: biliary (retained CBD stone, bile duct injury, cystic duct stump stone, sphincter of Oddi dysfunction) and non-biliary (IBS, GERD, peptic ulcer, pancreatic disease)

landmarkTrials.trial: Early vs Delayed Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy for Acute Cholecystitis (2006, JAMA — ACDC Trial)
landmarkTrials.population: 618 patients with acute cholecystitis randomized to early (<24h) vs delayed (7-12 weeks) laparoscopic cholecystectomy
landmarkTrials.intervention: Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy (within 24h of presentation) vs delayed surgery (after 7-12 weeks interval)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Early: complication rate 6% vs delayed 12% (p=0.01); conversion to open 10% vs 16% (p=0.03); total hospital stay 5 vs 9 days (p<0.001). Early cholecystectomy superior to delayed.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Supports early cholecystectomy in Kenyan practice — reduces total hospital stay and loss to follow-up

landmarkTrials.trial: Cholecystectomy vs Cholecystostomy for High-Risk Patients (CHOCOLATE Trial, 2018, JAMA)
landmarkTrials.population: 142 high-risk patients (APACHE II >7) with acute cholecystitis randomized to laparoscopic cholecystectomy vs percutaneous cholecystostomy
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy (within 24h) vs percutaneous cholecystostomy with delayed cholecystectomy if needed
landmarkTrials.outcome: Laparoscopic group: lower mortality (2% vs 12%), fewer reinterventions (2% vs 54%), shorter hospital stay (8 vs 14 days). Cholecystectomy superior even in high-risk patients.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Challenged dogma of cholecystostomy for high-risk patients; cholecystectomy preferred if feasible

landmarkTrials.trial: Laparoscopic vs Open Cholecystectomy for Acute Cholecystitis (2010, Annals of Surgery — Multicenter RCT)
landmarkTrials.population: 412 patients with acute cholecystitis randomized to laparoscopic vs open cholecystectomy
landmarkTrials.outcome: Laparoscopic: shorter hospital stay (4 vs 7 days), less pain, faster return to work (14 vs 28 days). Similar bile duct injury rate (0.5% vs 0.5%). Conversion to open: 12%.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy standard of care; open remains backup in difficult cases

landmarkTrials.trial: Antibiotics vs No Antibiotics After Cholecystectomy (PENNY Trial, 2019, Annals of Surgery)
landmarkTrials.population: 414 patients with mild cholecystitis randomized to postop antibiotics vs no antibiotics after cholecystectomy
landmarkTrials.intervention: Postoperative antibiotics (3 days) vs no antibiotics after early laparoscopic cholecystectomy
landmarkTrials.outcome: No difference in infectious complications (7% vs 8%). Postoperative antibiotics not needed in mild cholecystitis after cholecystectomy.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Single preoperative antibiotic dose sufficient for Grade I; reduces antibiotic use and resistance

landmarkTrials.trial: Early vs Interval Cholecystectomy for Cholecystitis (2020, The Lancet — SECURE Trial)
landmarkTrials.population: 691 patients with acute cholecystitis randomized to early cholecystectomy (<7 days) vs interval (>6 weeks)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Early cholecystectomy vs delayed cholecystectomy after 6 weeks
landmarkTrials.outcome: Early group: 97% completed surgery within index admission vs 30% in delayed group (70% did not return for delayed surgery). Major complications similar. Early surgery prevents loss to follow-up.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Early cholecystectomy critical in Kenya to prevent loss to follow-up; delayed surgery has high dropout rate

landmarkTrials.trial: Percutaneous Cholecystostomy Outcomes (2015, Journal of the American College of Surgeons)
landmarkTrials.population: 572 patients with acute cholecystitis treated with percutaneous cholecystostomy (multicenter registry)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Clinical success 85%; 30-day mortality 5%; drain-related complications 10%; recurrence 10% within 6 months; 50% eventually underwent cholecystectomy.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Cholecystostomy is a temporizing measure; definitive cholecystectomy needed for most patients

landmarkTrials.trial: Subtotal Cholecystectomy for Difficult Gallbladder (2016, Annals of Surgery — Systematic Review)
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 20 studies on subtotal cholecystectomy (n=2,100)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Subtotal cholecystectomy (deliberately leaving part of gallbladder wall) vs total cholecystectomy for difficult cases
landmarkTrials.outcome: Subtotal: lower bile duct injury (0.2% vs 0.8%) but higher bile leak (5% vs 1%). Safe alternative when Calot's triangle dissection is hazardous. Reconstituting vs fenestrating approaches have similar outcomes.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Subtotal cholecystectomy is an important bail-out technique for difficult cases in Kenyan practice

landmarkTrials.trial: TG18 Severity Grading Validation for Cholecystitis (2018, Journal of Hepato-Biliary-Pancreatic Sciences)
landmarkTrials.population: 7,500+ patients with acute cholecystitis — validation of Tokyo Guidelines
landmarkTrials.intervention: Validation of TG18 severity grading (Grade I, II, III) against outcomes
landmarkTrials.outcome: Grade I: 0.5% mortality, Grade II: 2% mortality, Grade III: 10% mortality. Grade III strongly predicts need for ICU, longer hospital stay, and higher mortality.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: TG18 grading applicable in Kenya; guides triage and timing of intervention

evidenceTable.recommendation: Early cholecystectomy within 72h of symptom onset

evidenceTable.therapy: Cholecystectomy vs Cholecystostomy in High-Risk (CHOCOLATE Trial)
evidenceTable.recommendation: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy first-line even in high-risk patients

evidenceTable.therapy: Laparoscopic vs Open Cholecystectomy for Acute Cholecystitis
evidenceTable.effect: Lap: 4 days vs Open: 7 days; return to work 14 vs 28 days
evidenceTable.recommendation: Laparoscopic preferred; open if conversion needed

evidenceTable.therapy: Antibiotics vs No Antibiotics Post-Cholecystectomy (PENNY Trial)
evidenceTable.recommendation: Single dose preoperative antibiotics sufficient for Grade I

evidenceTable.endpoint: Bile duct injury and bile leak
evidenceTable.effect: BDI 0.2% vs 0.8%; bile leak 5% vs 1% vs total chole
evidenceTable.recommendation: Subtotal cholecystectomy safe bail-out when Calot's triangle unsafe
Temporizing measure; definitive cholecystectomy needed for most

evidenceTable.effect: Grade I: 0.5%, Grade II: 2%, Grade III: 10%
evidenceTable.recommendation: Use TG18 grading for triage and treatment planning

evidenceTable.therapy: Emergency vs Urgent vs Elective Cholecystectomy Timing
evidenceTable.effect: <24h: lowest conversion; 24-72h: similar outcomes; >72h: highest conversion and complication rates

evidenceTable.therapy: Culture-based vs Empiric Antibiotics in Cholecystitis
evidenceTable.population: Moderate-severe cholecystitis with positive bile cultures
evidenceTable.recommendation: Bile culture at cholecystectomy to guide antibiotics if infection suspected

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical suspicion — constant RUQ pain >6 hours, positive Murphy sign, fever, nausea/vomiting, pain after fatty meals, referred right shoulder pain

Step 2: Abdominal ultrasound — first-line imaging. Diagnostic criteria: gallbladder wall thickening >4 mm, pericholecystic fluid, gallbladder distension (long axis >10 cm), sonographic Murphy sign (probe pressure reproduces pain), gallstones or sludge

Step 3: Laboratory — CBC (leukocytosis with left shift), CRP (markedly elevated), LFTs (obstructive pattern: ALP/GGT elevation suggests CBD involvement), amylase/lipase (exclude pancreatitis), bilirubin (elevated in Mirizzi or CBD stone)

Step 4: Assess severity using TG18 criteria — Grade I (mild): no organ dysfunction, no local complications; Grade II (moderate): WBC >18K, palpable mass, duration >72h; Grade III (severe): organ dysfunction in any system
Step 5: Urgent CT abdomen with IV contrast — if US inconclusive, suspect complications (gangrene, emphysematous, perforation, abscess), or to differentiate from pancreatitis/malignancy
Step 6: MRCP or EUS — if biliary obstruction suspected (elevated bilirubin, dilated CBD, abnormal LFTs); identify CBD stones or assess for Mirizzi syndrome before surgical planning
Step 7: HIDA scan (cholescintigraphy) — gold standard when US equivocal; non-visualization of gallbladder = cystic duct obstruction (97% sensitivity); rarely needed when US diagnostic
Step 8: ECG — rule out inferior wall myocardial infarction (presents with epigastric/RUQ pain in 5% of MI cases); essential in older patients with cardiac risk factors
Step 9: Blood cultures — before antibiotics if febrile or moderate-severe (Grade II-III); positive in 30-50%; E. coli most common; guide antibiotic de-escalation
Step 10: Treatment decision — Grade I: early laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Grade II: urgent cholecystectomy after resuscitation. Grade III: ICU + cholecystostomy + delayed cholecystectomy

Kenya adaptation: US widely available even at district hospitals; laparoscopic cholecystectomy available at most county referral hospitals; open cholecystectomy remains standard if laparoscopic equipment unavailable
managementPearls: Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy (<72 hours from symptom onset) is superior to delayed — do NOT wait for the inflammation to 'cool down'; early surgery is easier because tissue planes are not yet fibrotic

The critical view of safety (CVS) is mandatory before clipping any structure — only two structures entering the gallbladder (cystic duct and cystic artery), cystic plate visible behind; if CVS cannot be achieved, perform subtotal cholecystectomy or convert to open

In severe inflammation where Calot's triangle is obliterated, use a fundus-first (dome-down) approach or subtotal cholecystectomy (leave the posterior wall adherent to liver bed) — this is safer than persisting with CVS dissection

Percutaneous cholecystostomy (US-guided drain) is rescue therapy for Grade III cholecystitis with organ dysfunction or for patients who are truly unfit for surgery — but 50% will eventually need cholecystectomy due to recurrence

In diabetes or elderly patients with cholecystitis, have a lower threshold for early surgery — they progress to gangrenous cholecystitis faster (within 24-48h) and have higher mortality from complications

LFTs and CBD diameter on US must be checked before cholecystectomy — a dilated CBD (>6 mm) or elevated ALP/bilirubin suggests CBD stones and may require preoperative MRCP/EUS or intraoperative cholangiogram
Intraoperative cholangiogram (IOC) should be performed when CBD anatomy is unclear, CBD stones are suspected, or to confirm safety of clipping — 'a picture of safety is worth a thousand clips'

Bile culture should be sent routinely in acute cholecystitis — positive cultures guide postoperative antibiotics if patient develops persistent fever or sepsis; E. coli and Klebsiella are the most common pathogens

Antibiotic prophylaxis: a single preoperative dose is sufficient for Grade I cholecystitis (PENNY trial); Grade II needs 3-5 days; Grade III needs 5-7 days; extend only if inadequate source control or bacteremia

Postoperative drain placement: selective — only when there is concerning oozing from the gallbladder bed, difficult dissection, suspected bile leak, or subtotal cholecystectomy; routine drainage prolongs hospital stay without benefit

Right upper quadrant pain after cholecystectomy (post-cholecystectomy syndrome) occurs in 10-30% — causes: retained CBD stone, bile duct injury, bile leak, cystic duct stump stone, sphincter of Oddi dysfunction, or pre-existing IBS — investigate systematically

Gallbladder polyps >10 mm or symptomatic polyps require cholecystectomy; polyps <5 mm can be observed with US at 6-monthly intervals; polyps 5-10 mm with growth on follow-up also warrant cholecystectomy due to malignant potential
operativeNuances: Patient positioning: supine, split-leg, steep reverse Trendelenburg (30-40°) with left lateral tilt (15-20°) — allows gravity to retract the duodenum and hepatic flexure away from the gallbladder; the surgeon stands between the patient's legs

Port placement: umbilical (10-12 mm, 30° laparoscope), epigastric (10-12 mm, right of falciform), right subcostal midclavicular (5 mm), right anterior axillary (5 mm); in obese patients, place ports higher (supraumbilical for camera, higher epigastric)

First entry: open (Hasson) technique at the umbilicus is safer than Veress needle in patients with previous surgery or when the gallbladder is distended and adherent to the abdominal wall — avoids bowel injury

Calot's triangle dissection: begin at the infundibulum-cystic duct junction; dissect the peritoneum on both the anterior and posterior surfaces of the infundibulum; push the fatty tissue of Calot's triangle downward to skeletonize the cystic duct and artery

Rouviere's sulcus (the sulcus between the right lobe and caudate lobe) is an essential landmark — the cystic duct always arises anterior to a plane drawn through Rouviere's sulcus; dissection must stay above this plane to avoid bile duct injury

Critical view of safety: rotate the infundibulum laterally (away from the CBD) to open Calot's triangle; the cystic plate (liver bed above the gallbladder) must be visible between the two structures entering the gallbladder; do not clip until this view is achieved

In acute cholecystitis with a tense, distended gallbladder, decompress by aspirating with a percutaneous needle (18G spinal needle through the fundus) — this allows a grasper to hold the gallbladder wall and improves visualization

Cystic duct closure: 2 proximal clips and 1 distal clip; ensure no CBD stones are present before clipping; if the cystic duct is broad (>5 mm), use an endoloop (pre-tied loop) or suture ligation — clips may not fully across a broad edematous duct

Gallbladder extraction: place in a retrieval bag (Endobag) to prevent bile spillage and port site seeding (if incidental cancer); extract through the umbilical port; if the gallbladder is too large, open the bag and aspirate stones before extraction

Subtotal cholecystectomy technique: leave the posterior wall of the gallbladder body adherent to the liver bed; close the infundibulum with a continuous suture (3-0 PDS) over a biliary balloon to prevent bile leak; do not attempt to find the cystic duct in a frozen Calot's triangle

Conversion to open: low threshold in resource-limited settings where advanced laparoscopic equipment may be lacking; indications: unclear anatomy, bleeding, Mirizzi syndrome, suspected bile duct injury, large stone impacted at neck, severe adhesions

prognosis.grade1MildUncomplicated: 0.5% mortality; full recovery with early cholecystectomy; hospital stay 2-4 days
prognosis.grade2ModerateComplicated: 2% mortality; may need longer antibiotics; conversion to open 15-20%; hospital stay 5-8 days
prognosis.grade3SevereOrganDysfunction: 10% mortality; ICU care needed; cholecystostomy may be required; delayed cholecystectomy in 6-12 weeks
prognosis.gangrenousEmphysematous: 15-25% mortality; surgical emergency; emergency cholecystectomy required; higher in diabetics
prognosis.acalculousCholecystitis: 30-50% mortality (driven by underlying critical illness); cholecystostomy preferred; cholecystectomy after recovery
prognosticFactors: TG18 severity grade — strongest single predictor of outcomes
Diabetes mellitus — increases risk of gangrenous cholecystitis 2-3 fold
Age >75 — independent predictor of higher complication and mortality rates
Delay in surgery >72 hours — higher conversion rate, more complications
Male sex — higher rate of complicated cholecystitis (gangrene, perforation)
WBC >18,000 or CRP >200 mg/L — markers of severe inflammation
CBD stone presence — adds complexity, may need ERCP, increases length of stay
Comorbidities (cirrhosis, CKD, CHF) increase surgical risk
prognosis.eastAfricanSpecific: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy increasingly available at county hospitals in Kenya; conversion to open common due to late presentation with severe inflammation; open cholecystectomy remains standard at district hospitals; patients often present with empyema or gangrene due to dietary transitions and delayed healthcare seeking

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Septic/gangrenous/perforated -> SEVERE (Grade III); simple inflamed gallbladder -> NON-SEVERE (Grade I-II)

history_questions.question: When did the right upper abdominal pain start? Is it constant?
Does the pain spread to your right shoulder or back?
Have you had a fever with chills?
Did the pain start after eating a heavy or fatty meal?
Have you had similar pain before that went away?
Do you feel nauseous or have you vomited?
Do you have known gallstones?
Have you noticed yellowing of your eyes or dark urine?
Have you ever been diagnosed with diabetes?
Are you pregnant or have you been pregnant recently?
Have you lost weight rapidly recently?
Do you eat a diet with fried foods, fatty meats, or oils?
Do you have any family members with gallstones?
Do you drink alcohol — beer, chang'aa, or busaa?
Have you had any previous abdominal surgeries?
Are you taking any medications including traditional herbs?
Do you have pain when breathing in deeply?
Do you have pain that has been going on for more than 6 hours continuously?
Do you have a personal or family history of gallstones?
Are you on hormone replacement therapy or oral contraceptives?
Have you had rapid weight loss through dieting or bariatric surgery?
Do you have cirrhosis, hepatitis, or other liver disease?
Have you had a previous episode of acute cholecystitis that resolved?
Have you been diagnosed with hemolytic conditions (sickle cell, thalassemia)?
Do you have diabetes, and if so, is your sugar well controlled?
Are you on total parenteral nutrition or have you been fasting for a long time?
Do you experience intolerance to fatty or fried foods?
Have you noticed a palpable lump or fullness under your right ribs?
Do you feel that you have been passing less urine than usual?
Have you had any abdominal surgeries in the past? (scar tissue may affect approach)
Do you have pain in your right shoulder or neck along with the abdominal pain?
Are you currently on any blood-thinning medications (warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel)?
Have you been experiencing episodes of nausea or vomiting with the pain?
Do you feel feverish or have you taken your temperature at home?
Do you have any allergies to antibiotics, especially penicillins or cephalosporins?
Are you pregnant? (pregnancy increases gallstone risk)

examination_findings.finding: Murphy's sign — inspiratory arrest on RUQ palpation (pathognomonic)
Right upper quadrant tenderness to palpation
Guarding or rebound in RUQ
Fever — temperature >37.8°C
Palpable gallbladder mass (distended gallbladder) in thin patients
Jaundice — suggests CBD involvement or Mirizzi syndrome
Tachycardia from pain, fever, or sepsis
Tachypnea — shallow breathing to avoid RUQ movement
Boas sign — right infrascapular referred hyperesthesia
Generalized peritonitis if perforation — rigidity, rebound, absent bowel sounds
Hypotension if gangrenous or septic
Dehydration signs — dry mucosa, reduced skin turgor
Right shoulder tip pain (referred phrenic nerve irritation)
Murphy sign (pathognomonic) — place hand on RUQ, ask patient to breathe in; inspiratory arrest = positive
Palpable, tender gallbladder mass — distended GB palpable below the right costal margin in thin patients
Fever (temperature >38°C) — more common with empyema or gangrenous cholecystitis
Jaundice suggests CBD involvement (Mirizzi syndrome, choledocholithiasis)
Tachycardia from pain, fever, dehydration, or sepsis
Tachypnea — shallow breathing to minimize diaphragmatic irritation from inflamed gallbladder
Boas sign: hyperesthesia over right infrascapular area (referred from phrenic nerve irritation)
Generalized peritonitis: guarding, rigidity, rebound, absent bowel sounds — indicates perforation
Abdominal distension from ileus in complicated cholecystitis
Hypotension and signs of shock — suggests gangrene, perforation, or associated cholangitis
Dehydration: dry oral mucosa, reduced skin turgor, sunken eyes
Cool, mottled peripheries with delayed capillary refill from sepsis
Subcostal or flank ecchymosis — rare, suggests retroperitoneal perforation or hemorrhage
Palpable lymph nodes in right axilla — rarely from advanced gallbladder cancer, not acute cholecystitis
Obesity (BMI >30) — common associated risk factor

bedside_scores.name: Tokyo Guidelines Severity Grade (TG18) for Cholecystitis

components.name: Grade II (Moderate)
components.score: WBC >18k, palpable mass, duration >72h, local complications

components.name: Grade III (Severe)
components.score: Organ dysfunction (CV, resp, renal, hepatic, neuro)

bedside_scores.interpretation: Grade I: early cholecystectomy. Grade II: urgent cholecystectomy or drainage. Grade III: ICU + drainage, delayed cholecystectomy.

riskFactors.note: Present in 90% of acute cholecystitis cases
Estrogen increases cholesterol saturation, gallbladder stasis

subtypes.name: Calculous (Stone-Related) Cholecystitis
subtypes.management: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 72 hours of symptom onset (early cholecystectomy). IV antibiotics: Ceftriaxone 2g daily + Metronidazole 500mg 8-hourly. Convert to open if severe inflammation, unclear anatomy, or Mirizzi syndrome.

subtypes.name: Acalculous (Without Stones) Cholecystitis
subtypes.management: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam or Carbapenem). Percutaneous cholecystostomy (US/CT-guided drain placement) if too ill for surgery. Delayed laparoscopic cholecystectomy after recovery from critical illness.

mimics.whyMimic: RUQ pain from pleural irritation, fever, tachypnea
RUQ pain, fever, tender hepatomegaly, leukocytosis
RUQ pain, fever, jaundice, hepatomegaly, leukocytosis
RUQ pain radiating to groin, nausea/vomiting

mimics.diseaseName: Sphincter of Oddi Dysfunction / Biliary Dyskinesia
mimics.whyMimic: Recurrent RUQ/epigastric pain after cholecystectomy or without stones
Sudden severe epigastric/RUQ pain, hypotension, shock

bedside.name: ECG
bedside.indication: Rule out inferior STEMI in atypical presentations

laboratory.name: WBC count with differential
laboratory.indication: Leukocytosis with left shift in bacterial cholecystitis

laboratory.name: CRP
laboratory.indication: Elevated CRP — marker of inflammation severity

laboratory.name: LFTs (AST, ALT, ALP, GGT, Bilirubin)
laboratory.indication: Obstructive pattern (↑ALP, GGT, bilirubin) suggests CBD stones

imaging.name: Right Upper Quadrant Ultrasound
imaging.indication: Gallbladder wall thickening >4mm, pericholecystic fluid, stones, Murphy on US

imaging.name: HIDA scan (Cholescintigraphy)
imaging.indication: Gold standard for diagnosis — non-visualization of GB = cystic duct obstruction

imaging.name: MRCP
imaging.indication: If CBD stones suspected or anatomy needs delineation

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV contrast
imaging.indication: If US inconclusive, assess for complications (gangrene, perforation, abscess)

resuscitation: IV fluids: Hartmann's or Normal Saline 1-2 L stat then 125 mL/hr
Analgesia: IV Paracetamol 1g 6-hourly, ± IV Morphine 2.5-5mg PRN
Avoid NSAIDs if elderly, dehydrated, or renal impairment
antibiotics: Duration: single dose preop or continue up to 5 days if severe
For Grade III or acalculous: Meropenem 1g IV 8-hourly

surgical.indication: All cases of acute cholecystitis — early cholecystectomy within 72 hours preferred
surgical.step1: Grade I: Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 72 hours of symptom onset
surgical.step2: Grade II: Urgent laparoscopic cholecystectomy after resuscitation; consider percutaneous drainage if local inflammation severe
surgical.step3: Grade III: ICU management, percutaneous cholecystostomy, delayed cholecystectomy in 6-12 weeks
surgical.approach: Laparoscopic (4-port technique). Convert to open if: unclear anatomy, Mirizzi, severe inflammation, bleeding

nonOperative: Percutaneous cholecystostomy: for Grade III, high surgical risk, acalculous cholecystitis
Antibiotics alone: for mild Grade I with rapid resolution
Delayed cholecystectomy: 6-12 weeks after resolution of acute episode

general: Advance diet as tolerated day 1: clear fluids, soft diet
Drain removal when output <30 mL/day serous
diet_advancement: Day 2: Soft diet (uji, porridge, yoghurt, mashed potatoes)
Day 3: Regular diet as tolerated — low fat initially

pathways.criteria: Mild inflammation, no organ dysfunction, no complications, WBC <18k
pathways.treatment: IV antibiotics (Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole). Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 72 hours.

pathways.criteria: WBC >18k, palpable tender mass, duration >72h, local complications
pathways.treatment: IV antibiotics. Urgent laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Consider cholecystostomy if high surgical risk.

pathways.criteria: Organ dysfunction (CV, respiratory, renal, hepatic, neurological)
pathways.treatment: ICU. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics. Organ support. Percutaneous cholecystostomy. Delayed cholecystectomy 6-12 weeks after recovery.

steps: Supine, split-leg position, reverse Trendelenburg, left tilt
4-port setup: umbilical camera (10-12mm), epigastric (10mm), right subcostal (5mm), right flank (5mm)
Identify and retract gallbladder fundus cephalad using fundal grasper
Retract gallbladder infundibulum laterally to expose Calot's triangle
Dissect Calot's triangle — identify cystic duct and cystic artery
Critical view of safety: only two structures entering gallbladder, cystic plate visible behind
Clip and divide cystic artery: 2 clips proximal, 1 distal
Clip and divide cystic duct: 2 clips proximal, 1 distal (or endoloop if broad)
Dissect gallbladder from liver bed using electrocautery or harmonic scalpel
Retrieve gallbladder in specimen bag through epigastric port
Place drain if oozing or severe inflammation
Close port sites — fascial closure at umbilical port
operative_templates.approach: Laparoscopic (4-port); convert to open if needed
operative_templates.position: Supine, split-leg, reverse Trendelenburg, left side down

immediate_postop_24h.complication: Bleeding from cystic artery or liver bed
Bile leak from accessory duct or cystic duct stump

dischargeCriteria: Pain controlled on oral analgesia (paracetamol, ibuprofen)
Tolerating soft diet without nausea or vomiting
Drain removed or output <30 mL/day serous (if placed)
Understands warning signs of bile duct injury or retained stone

patient_education.explanation: Acute cholecystitis is inflammation of the gallbladder — a small pouch under your liver that stores bile. It is usually caused by a gallstone blocking the outlet of the gallbladder. This causes pain, fever, and inflammation. The standard treatment is surgery to remove the gallbladder (cholecystectomy). You can live normally without a gallbladder.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital if: you develop worsening abdominal pain, fever or chills, yellowing of your eyes or skin, bile-colored drainage from your wound, or persistent vomiting.
patient_education.activity: After laparoscopic surgery, you can walk the same day. Avoid heavy lifting (>5 kg) for 2 weeks. You can return to work in 1-2 weeks (desk job) or 3-4 weeks (manual labor). No driving for 1 week after surgery.
patient_education.diet: Start with light foods after surgery: clear soups, porridge (uji), tea. Gradually add solid foods. After recovery, eat a healthy low-fat diet. Some people experience looser stools after gallbladder removal — this usually improves with time. Avoid very fatty, fried foods in the first weeks. Small frequent meals are better than large meals.
patient_education.medication: Take antibiotics as prescribed (usually 5 days). Pain medication as needed (paracetamol). Avoid NSAIDs if you have kidney problems or are elderly.
patient_education.followUp: Return for wound check at 2 weeks. No routine follow-up needed if asymptomatic. If you had jaundice before surgery or abnormal LFTs, schedule MRCP/ERCP if needed.

after_discharge.action: Surgical clinic review — wound check, histology results
If CBD stone was suspected: MRCP or EUS
Return to normal activities, work clearance

when_to_return: Increasing abdominal pain, distension, or vomiting
Persistent diarrhea lasting >2 weeks after surgery
